title: Meta Cut 8,000 Jobs While Doubling Down on AI
metaDescription: Meta just laid off 8,000 people but poured billions into AI. The workers who know automation aren't going anywhere.
author: Marty @ CertFlo

sections.heading: Meta Cut 8,000 People But Hired AI Instead
sections.body: Meta laid off 8,000 employees while dumping billions into AI development. The Indian Express says they're cutting costs to fund massive AI investments. This isn't just about layoffs though. Meta figured out what every company is gonna figure out: some jobs AI does better. Some jobs still need humans. They're keeping the humans who work with AI and cutting the ones competing against it.

sections.heading: Two Types of Workers Now
sections.body:
Here's what's happening at Meta and everywhere else. Companies are sorting people into two buckets.

Bucket one: workers doing stuff AI can replace tomorrow.
Bucket two: workers who already use AI to get more done.

The people getting cut? They were in bucket one. The ones staying? They figured out how to multiply their output with AI tools.

It's not really about being smart or working harder. It's about which side of the divide you're on.

sections.heading: This Creates Space for People Who Get It
sections.body:
Every company watching Meta is asking the same thing: how do we not get left behind?

The answer isn't cutting more people. It's finding workers who can do 10x more by mixing human judgment with AI tools.

Think about it. While 8,000 people are job hunting, there's millions of spots opening up for people who understand AI automation. Marketing that used to take weeks now takes hours if you know the right tools. Data analysis that needed whole teams can be done by one person with the right AI skills.

The companies making these moves aren't just surviving. They're winning.

sections.heading: The Gap Is Wide Open Right Now
sections.body:
idk man, I've been watching workplace tech for years. Never seen a skill gap this obvious.

Most workers are still figuring out AI through trial and error. Wasting time on bad prompts. Missing the stuff that actually matters.

But the workers who learned this systematically? They're becoming impossible to replace.

They're the marketing people launching campaigns in days instead of months. The analysts processing datasets that would take normal teams weeks. The project managers who automated half their workflows so their teams can focus on the stuff that actually needs humans.

This isn't some future thing. It's happening right now.

sections.heading: The Window Is Right Now
sections.body: Meta's move sends a pretty clear signal. The AI shift isn't coming anymore. It's here. Question isn't whether AI changes your industry. It's whether you're leading that change or just watching it happen. Every day someone else is building the AI skills that make them irreplaceable. Learning the automation stuff that 10x their output. Positioning themselves as the bridge between human strategy and AI execution — exactly what you'll find in this comprehensive career advancement playbook.
